reference: The Central Bank of Sri Lanka issued Circular No. 04 of 2024 to provide relief measures for Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) affected by the Easter Sunday attacks, the COVID-19 pandemic, and recent adverse macroeconomic conditions. The circular applies to licensed commercial banks and licensed specialized banks and establishes a framework for assisting eligible SME borrowers through loan restructuring and other financial relief measures. Eligible borrowers include SMEs whose credit facilities were classified as Stage 3 on or after 1 April 2019 and who commence discussions with their bank's Business Revival Unit by 31 March 2025 while submitting the required supporting documents. Licensed banks are required to assess each borrower's repayment capacity and business revival plan before entering into loan rescheduling agreements. Such agreements must be finalized no later than 15 June 2025. The circular also specifies different repayment commencement deadlines depending on the outstanding capital amount as of 15 December 2024. In addition to loan rescheduling, licensed banks are expected to waive unpaid interest accrued between 1 April 2019 and 15 December 2024 according to the percentages specified in the circular, taking into account the outstanding loan amount and settlement period. Banks may also provide additional relief, including working capital facilities, where justified by the borrower's repayment capacity and credible business revival plan. Regarding credit reporting, banks may develop an appropriate reporting mechanism with the Credit Information Bureau of Sri Lanka (CRIB) for restructured facilities and must not reject loan applications solely due to an adverse CRIB record if the borrower is eligible under this circular. To ensure transparency, banks must communicate reasons for any rejection of relief requests, inform borrowers of their right to appeal to the Financial Consumer Relations Department of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka, and provide a breakdown of capital, interest, and other charges upon request. Overall, the circular establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework to support the recovery of financially distressed SMEs while promoting consistency, transparency, and responsible credit restructuring across licensed banks.

The Central Bank of Sri Lanka issued Circular No. 01 of 2025 as an addendum to Circular No. 04 of 2024 to clarify and strengthen the implementation of relief measures provided to affected Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs). The objective of this addendum is to ensure that all licensed banks apply the relief measures consistently and effectively across the banking sector. The circular requires licensed banks to establish a dedicated Relief Banking Unit responsible for extending, monitoring, and administering the relief measures granted under the original circular. This unit is intended to work alongside existing Business Revival Units established under Circular No. 02 of 2024, which will continue to operate for their original purpose. A significant amendment introduced through the addendum is the insertion of a new Paragraph 1.4 under the features of the relief measures. Under this provision, licensed banks may reschedule credit facilities of eligible borrowers for a maximum period of up to ten years, unless the original credit agreement already provides a longer period. Such rescheduling decisions must be made on a case-by-case basis, taking into account the borrower's repayment capacity and the existence of an acceptable business revival plan. The circular further states that both the licensed bank and the eligible borrower must mutually agree on the rescheduling terms and conditions, including the applicable interest rate, which should be determined with consideration to prevailing benchmark interest rates. In addition, the circular introduces a new Paragraph 3.3 relating to transparency in the provision of relief measures. Licensed banks are instructed to establish a transparent grievance-handling mechanism to address disputes that may arise between banks and borrowers regarding the valuation of auctioned properties. The addendum therefore strengthens governance, transparency, and consistency in the implementation of SME relief measures while providing greater flexibility for restructuring credit facilities and improving dispute resolution processes.

The Central Bank of Sri Lanka issued Circular No. 01 of 2021 to provide temporary relief for Small and Medium Enterprise (SME) Paddy Millers affected by the COVID-19 pandemic. The circular requires all licensed commercial banks and licensed specialized banks to suspend recovery actions against eligible SME Paddy Millers for a period of six months commencing from 1 January 2021. Where banks have already initiated recovery proceedings under the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act, the Mortgage Act, the Finance Leasing Act, or any other applicable legislation, such recovery actions must be suspended provided that the borrower and the bank enter into a mutually agreed debt repayment arrangement. The circular also instructs banks to defer passing new resolutions for loan recovery under the relevant Acts. In situations where recovery resolutions have already been passed, banks are required to suspend the auctioning of assets until 30 June 2021. Furthermore, where legal proceedings relating to loan recovery are already underway in courts, borrowers may enter into repayment agreements by submitting affidavits to the courts confirming their commitment to comply with the agreed repayment arrangements. In addition to suspending recovery proceedings under specific Acts, licensed banks are also directed to suspend any other legal or recovery actions against eligible SME Paddy Millers until 30 June 2021. The objective of the circular is to provide temporary financial relief to SME Paddy Millers during the COVID-19 pandemic, allowing affected businesses additional time to recover while encouraging negotiated debt repayment agreements between borrowers and banks. The circular supports government initiatives aimed at protecting the agricultural sector and maintaining the continuity of paddy milling operations while balancing the interests of both borrowers and licensed banks.

The Central Bank of Sri Lanka issued Circular No. 01 of 2022 to establish Post COVID-19 Revival Units in all licensed commercial banks and licensed specialized banks with the objective of supporting businesses affected by the COVID-19 pandemic. The circular aims to revive viable businesses experiencing financial distress due to reduced income, impaired business operations, or business closures while minimizing the growth of non-performing loans in the banking sector. Every licensed bank is required to establish a dedicated Post COVID-19 Revival Unit and formulate a Board-approved revival and rehabilitation policy outlining the unit's objectives, governance structure, responsibilities, and rehabilitation mechanisms. The Revival Unit must be headed by a senior key management officer, adequately staffed, and empowered to identify eligible borrowers, assess financial conditions, coordinate rehabilitation strategies, and monitor recovery progress. Rehabilitation measures may include restructuring or rescheduling existing credit facilities, granting additional working capital, providing business advisory services, arranging investor support, and offering credit counselling. The circular excludes willful defaulters, borrowers involved in fraud, diversion of funds, or unviable business projects from revival assistance, while allowing banks to consider certain non-performing borrowers and borrowers under litigation at their discretion. Banks are also required to adopt appropriate accounting treatments in accordance with Sri Lanka Accounting Standards and establish a reporting mechanism with the Credit Information Bureau of Sri Lanka (CRIB) to ensure participation in revival programs does not negatively affect future credit reports. Furthermore, banks must establish these Revival Units by 30 April 2022 and submit quarterly progress reports to the Central Bank within 15 working days after the end of each quarter beginning from 30 June 2022. Overall, the circular provides a comprehensive governance framework for helping viable businesses recover from the economic effects of the COVID-19 pandemic while strengthening credit risk management and promoting sustainable economic recovery.

The Central Bank of Sri Lanka issued Circular No. 02 of 2022 to provide broad guidelines for licensed commercial banks and licensed specialized banks to grant concessions to borrowers affected by the prevailing extraordinary macroeconomic circumstances. The circular aims to assist businesses and individuals experiencing financial difficulties while maintaining the stability of the banking sector. Licensed banks are required to assess borrowers on a case-by-case basis and provide suitable repayment arrangements based on their revised repayment capacity. Eligible concessions for performing credit facilities include grace periods for capital or interest, restructuring or rescheduling of loans, and other repayment relief for a period of six months from the date of the circular. Banks are also required to structure repayment plans without increasing contracted installment values and may apply concessionary interest rates within the limits specified in the circular. Borrowers wishing to settle facilities early must be allowed to do so without early settlement charges, and banks may also grant rebates where appropriate. For non-performing credit facilities, banks may restructure loans over extended periods, suspend recovery actions including parate execution, defer legal recovery proceedings, postpone asset auctions until 31 December 2022, and suspend other legal recovery actions where repayment agreements are reached. However, willful defaulters, borrowers involved in fraud, diversion of funds, or unviable projects are excluded from these concessions. The circular further requires banks not to reject loan applications solely due to adverse CRIB records and to establish appropriate reporting arrangements with the Credit Information Bureau of Sri Lanka. Banks must follow Sri Lanka Accounting Standards for accounting treatment of concessions and ensure transparency by informing borrowers of decisions within one month, providing reasons for rejected requests, allowing appeals to the Financial Consumer Relations Department of the Central Bank, and obtaining borrower consent before implementing restructuring arrangements. Licensed banks are also required to submit monthly reports on concessions granted to the Bank Supervision Department. Overall, the circular establishes a comprehensive framework for borrower relief while ensuring prudent risk management, transparency, and consistency across the banking sector.

Circular No. 04 of 2018, issued by the Bank Supervision Department of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 31 December 2018, establishes minimum guidelines for the consistent and prudent adoption of Sri Lanka Accounting Standard – SLFRS 9: Financial Instruments by licensed banks in Sri Lanka. It applies to all licensed banks and places responsibility for the preparation, presentation and disclosure of compliant financial statements on their Boards of Directors and senior management. The guidelines cover classification and measurement of financial assets and liabilities, business-model assessments, the Solely Payments of Principal and Interest test, fair-value measurement, credit-risk management, impairment, collateral valuation, internal audit, regulatory reporting and disclosures. Banks must maintain documented and Board-approved business models, standardised assessment processes, robust expected credit-loss models, adequate systems and reliable data. Reclassifications arising from changes in business models require Board approval and must be notified to the Director of Bank Supervision within seven working days. Expected credit losses must be calculated for applicable amortised-cost and FVOCI assets, loan commitments, financial guarantees, lease receivables and contract assets. Lifetime expected credit losses apply where credit risk has increased significantly, while 12-month expected credit losses apply where it has not. Significant credit-risk indicators include payments more than 30 days past due, rating deterioration, economic or geographical changes, collateral deterioration, litigation, restructuring, insolvency and specified declines in turnover, profit or net worth. Stage 3 generally includes payments more than 90 days past due and qualifying non-performing, rescheduled or repeatedly restructured facilities. Banks must ordinarily use at least five years of data for probability-of-default calculations. Proxies are allowed only for newly incorporated banks with less than five years of adequate credit history. Where LGD cannot be computed due to insufficient information, a minimum LGD of 45 per cent applies. Special LGD provisions include zero per cent for qualifying same-currency cash-backed loans, 20 per cent for foreign-currency sovereign exposures, and specified treatment for Government-guaranteed exposures. Credit models must be independently validated and reviewed at least annually. Regulatory returns remain subject to existing requirements, while Tables 4 and 5 must be submitted to the Bank Supervision Department within 30 days after each quarter, commencing 31 December 2018. First-day SLFRS 9 impacts must be disclosed and absorbed for capital adequacy purposes over the prescribed four-year transition. No penalties, appeal process, customer repayment schedule or recovery procedure is specified. Overall, the circular creates a comprehensive governance, measurement, reporting and control framework for SLFRS 9 compliance.

Circular No. 02 of 2021, issued by the Bank Supervision Department of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 25 January 2021, amends Circular No. 04 of 2018 concerning the Guidelines to Licensed Banks on the Adoption of Sri Lanka Accounting Standard – SLFRS 9: Financial Instruments. The objective of the amendment is to support consistent practices among licensed banks in applying SLFRS 9 under the current exceptional circumstances and in light of the latest Budget proposal relating to International Sovereign Bonds. The circular applies to licensed banks and therefore affects institutions required to calculate and recognize expected losses under SLFRS 9. The circular does not identify any separate class of individual beneficiaries or eligible customers; the eligible parties for applying the amendment are licensed banks that compute expected losses for the year 2021. The principal requirement is the insertion of a new Guideline 2.5(c) immediately after Guideline 2.5(b) in Annex I of Circular No. 04 of 2018. The inserted provision permits licensed banks to apply a minimum loss given default, or LGD, of 10 per cent when computing expected losses for the year 2021. This is a permitted minimum rather than a mandatory fixed LGD for every exposure. Implementation requires banks to incorporate Guideline 2.5(c) into their application of the existing SLFRS 9 guidelines and ensure that any use of the 10 per cent minimum LGD is limited to expected-loss computations for 2021. The circular does not prescribe a separate implementation date beyond its issuance date, an acknowledgement deadline, a submission deadline, or a transitional procedure. It also does not specify any reporting format, regulatory return, certification, or notification that must be submitted to the Central Bank of Sri Lanka. Nevertheless, licensed banks remain responsible for complying with the amended Annex I and maintaining consistent SLFRS 9 practices. No penalties, enforcement measures, appeal procedures, interest-rate rules, repayment requirements, recovery actions, or customer-level exclusions are stated. The special condition is that the permission applies specifically to the computation of expected losses for the year 2021 and establishes a minimum LGD of 10 per cent. In conclusion, the circular provides a narrowly defined, time-bound amendment intended to facilitate consistent expected-loss measurement by licensed banks during exceptional circumstances.

Circular No. 02 of 2025, issued by the Bank Supervision Department of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 07 May 2025, establishes mandatory procedures for reporting Information Technology and cybersecurity incidents by licensed commercial banks and licensed specialised banks. Its objective is to ensure prompt and transparent reporting, enable the Central Bank of Sri Lanka and relevant stakeholders to assess risks, mitigate service disruptions, protect customer information and assets, and strengthen the operational resilience of licensed banks. The circular supplements the Banking Act Direction No. 16 of 2021 on the Regulatory Framework on Technology Risk Management and Resilience for Licensed Banks, as amended. Reportable events include intrusion or hacking, malware, ransomware, malicious code, viruses, phishing, Distributed Denial of Service attacks, social engineering, unauthorised system usage, insider threats, Advanced Persistent Threats, supply-chain attacks, online or digital scams affecting customers, unplanned critical system outages, interruptions, failures, slowness or unresponsiveness, regulatory non-compliances relating to IT and cybersecurity, and any other technology or cyber-related event. Licensed banks must submit an immediate report using Annex 01 within two hours of detecting an incident, based on the information available at that time. A detailed report using Annex 02, together with supporting documents, must then be submitted. Table 01 states a timeline of within 14 days of detection, while the detailed reporting guidance in Annex 02 specifies within 14 working days; this difference should be clarified with the regulator rather than assumed. Quarterly reports under Annex 03 must be submitted within 15 days following the end of each quarter. Reports must be addressed to the Director of the Bank Supervision Department through techrisk.bsd@cbsl.lk, with dbsd@cbsl.lk copied. Reporting must include incident details, root cause, internal escalation, other authorities notified, customer or stakeholder communications, business and security impacts, legal and regulatory implications, financial losses, corrective actions, target completion dates and, for quarterly reporting, amounts recovered. Incidents may be escalated internally to the Chief Information Security Officer, Chief Information Officer, Chief Executive Officer, Information Security Committee, Board Integrated Risk Management Committee and Board of Directors. Relevant external authorities may include SLCERT, FinCSIRT, the CBSL Payment and Settlement Department, CCID and other legal authorities. No acknowledgement deadline, interest rate, repayment requirement, appeal process or specific statutory penalty is prescribed. The earlier circular dated 25 January 2016 on Reporting on Cybersecurity Events is revoked with immediate effect.

Circular No. 03 of 2021, issued by the Monetary Board of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 10 March 2021, introduces concessions for lease facilities obtained by COVID-19-affected businesses and individuals engaged in passenger transportation services. The objective is to address financial difficulties arising from the pandemic and ensure consistent implementation of the relief scheme across licensed commercial banks and licensed specialised banks. Eligible borrowers include businesses and individuals providing public passenger transportation, school and office transport services, taxis including three-wheelers, and passenger transportation services connected to the tourism sector. Banks may request documentary evidence confirming that the relevant vehicle is used for an eligible passenger transportation activity. The concession applies to performing lease facilities, including facilities already under moratorium as at 31 March 2021. Licensed banks must defer both capital and interest instalments for up to six months commencing 1 April 2021, or for a shorter appropriate period based on the borrower’s financial difficulties. Eligible borrowers were expected to apply in writing or electronically on or before 19 April 2021. Applications received after that date must also be accepted where the reason for delay is acceptable. Banks must communicate the concession, application deadline and application format through printed or electronic channels, including email and SMS. The existing lease tenure must be extended by the deferment period. Interest charged for the deferred period must not exceed the latest auction rate for 364-day Treasury Bills available as at 1 April 2021 plus one per cent per annum. Such interest is generally recoverable from April 2023, although earlier recovery may begin when the remaining lease tenure ends before April 2023. The circular also establishes sequencing for recovering deferred instalments and amounts due under earlier moratoriums. Borrowers may request early settlement, in which case the bank and borrower must agree on the structure, tenure and a current market interest rate. Banks may offer alternative arrangements only where benefits are not less favourable than those under the circular and the interest computation is clearly explained. Penal interest accrued and unpaid as at 1 April 2021 must be waived, no penal interest may accrue during deferment, and excessive fees or charges are prohibited. Declined requests must include reasons. Banks must account for concessions under applicable Sri Lanka Accounting Standards and report monthly deferment details to the Bank Supervision Department within 15 working days, commencing 1 May 2021. No specific penalty or formal appeal procedure is stated.

Circular No. 03 of 2024, issued by the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 03 December 2024, establishes uniform requirements for the establishment and operation of Mobile Banking Units by licensed commercial banks. Its objective is to strengthen the approval process, governance arrangements, risk controls and operational practices applicable to such units. Under section 13A(1) of the Banking Act, No. 30 of 1988, as amended, the Deputy Governor may approve the establishment of Mobile Banking Units subject to applicable terms and conditions. The circular applies only to licensed commercial banks and covers banking services provided through vehicles at different locations, barefoot banking involving officers visiting customers outside bank premises, units operating at permanent locations on selected days of the week, and temporary or ad-hoc outlets at public locations such as schools, temples, carnivals, exhibitions and conferences. Each Mobile Banking Unit must operate under a Board-approved framework or operating guidelines. At a minimum, the framework must define the scope of activities, deposit and withdrawal limits, accounting and reporting procedures, system-access controls, segregation of duties, the responsible affiliated branch, Customer Charter compliance, logistics and regulatory approvals, security and insurance arrangements, and management reporting. Permitted activities include accepting deposits and withdrawals, opening accounts subject to Know Your Customer and Customer Due Diligence requirements, accepting loan and credit-card applications, receiving repayments and card payments, promoting banking products, onboarding existing customers to digital channels, facilitating utility payments and providing advisory services. Other incidental services require authorization by the Deputy Governor. Licensed commercial banks intending to conduct Mobile Banking Unit activities during an upcoming quarter must submit the completed BSD-MBU-01 application to the Director of Bank Supervision at least 15 working days before the beginning of that quarter. The application must be accompanied by a cover letter signed by the Chief Executive Officer, Chief Operating Officer, Deputy Chief Executive Officer or relevant Deputy General Manager. The application requires information on the location, purpose, operating period, affiliated branch, approvals, staff, services, machines, security, insurance, communication arrangements, real-time account updates and internal guidelines. Mobile Banking Unit accounts must be incorporated into the affiliated branch’s general ledger on a real-time basis, except for units established at schools for students. The bank and affiliated branch names must also be displayed in all three languages. No penalties, appeal procedures, interest rates, repayment schedules or recovery actions are specified.

Circular No. 08 of 2021, issued by the Monetary Board of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 1 September 2021, extends concessions previously granted under Circular No. 05 of 2021 to businesses and individuals affected by the renewed COVID-19 outbreak. It applies to licensed commercial banks and licensed specialised banks and aims to ease repayment pressure caused by loss of employment, reduced income or sales, impaired operations and business closures. Borrowers in the tourism sector are excluded because separate tourism-sector concessions apply. For performing credit facilities as at 1 September 2021, banks must consider deferring capital, interest or both, on a case-by-case basis, up to 31 December 2021, with priority given to Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise borrowers. The relief may apply to one or more Rupee or foreign-currency facilities based on financial difficulty and repayment capacity. Amounts due under previous and current moratoriums must be combined into a new loan with a separate amortisation schedule. For Rupee facilities, the interest rate must not exceed the 364-day Treasury Bill auction rate as at 31 August 2021 plus one per cent, namely 6.93 per cent. Foreign-currency facilities may carry a concessionary rate. Existing facilities resume repayment from 1 January 2022, while repayment of the new loan for instalment and lease facilities may commence from 1 July 2022 for up to six months, unless an earlier date is agreed. Longer repayment periods require an agreed concessionary rate. Revolving facilities falling due between 1 September and 31 December 2021 may be extended. Penal interest must not accrue during that concessionary period, and lockdown-related delays of up to 15 working days must be allowed without additional interest or charges. Non-performing facilities may be rescheduled based on repayment capacity and an acceptable revival plan. Penal interest accrued from 1 April 2020 to 1 September 2021 must be waived where restructuring is granted, and recovery action against facilities classified non-performing on or after 1 April 2020 must be suspended until 31 December 2021. Applications were due by 21 September 2021, although late requests may be accepted for acceptable reasons. Rejections should be communicated preferably within 14 days, with reasons and appeal rights to the Financial Consumer Relations Department. Monthly reports are due within 15 working days, commencing 30 September 2021.

Circular No. 10 of 2021, issued by the Monetary Board of the Central Bank of Sri Lanka on 13 September 2021, extends the debt moratorium previously granted under Circular No. 08 of 2020 to COVID-19-affected businesses and individuals in the tourism industry. The objective is to address continuing repayment difficulties faced by the tourism sector and ensure consistent implementation across licensed commercial banks and licensed specialised banks. Eligible parties include tourism-sector businesses, affected employees of eligible businesses and members of eligible associations. For employees and association members, registration of the relevant business or association with the institutions specified under the earlier scheme is considered sufficient. The moratorium applies to both capital and interest for a further nine-month period from 1 October 2021 to 30 June 2022. Eligible borrowers were required to request the extension on or before 15 October 2021, although banks may accept late requests where the reason for delay is acceptable. Borrowers who retain the capacity to service their loans are expected to continue repayment rather than request relief. Banks must convert capital and interest falling due during the extended moratorium into a term loan and amalgamate these amounts with capital and interest deferred under the previous moratorium. Recovery of the converted loan may commence after the extended moratorium ends. Accrued and unpaid penal interest as at 1 October 2021 on eligible performing loans must be waived, and no penal interest may accrue during the moratorium period. Banks may alternatively restructure facilities over a longer period based on repayment capacity and an acceptable revival plan, with mutually agreed terms including a concessionary interest rate. Borrowers choosing early settlement should be considered for interest rebates, while early-settlement fees, other charges and future interest on lease facilities must be waived where applicable. All recovery actions against facilities classified as non-performing on or after 1 April 2020 must be suspended until 30 June 2022. Banks must avoid excessive contact, pressure or visits during collection, while borrowers involved in court proceedings must enter into an agreement before the court to receive the concession. Tourism must be treated as a risk-elevated sector under SLFRS 9, with appropriate impairment provisioning. Monthly moratorium reports must continue to be submitted to the Director of Bank Supervision as at the 30th of each month within 15 working days. Rejections should be communicated preferably within 14 days, with reasons and appeal rights to the Financial Consumer Relations Department.

Human Resource Division Circular No. 65/2025, issued by Bank of Ceylon on 12 August 2025, communicates the Board-approved revised Human Resource Policy on Remote Work, effective from 14 July 2025. The policy establishes the conditions, procedures and controls governing employees who perform official duties from approved locations outside their normal designated workplace. It applies to eligible Bank of Ceylon employees but excludes categories and positions that are not remote compliant. Remote work may be occasional, temporary or permanent and may be initiated by either management or an employee. However, it requires prior management approval, satisfaction of the Remote Work Approval Checklist and execution of a written Remote Work Agreement. Retrospective requests are generally not accepted, and work performed from an unauthorised location is not recognised as remote work. Remote workers must use an approved Remote Work Location and designated workspace that satisfies safety, electrical, fire-protection, ergonomic, productivity, equipment and information-security standards. Unless otherwise agreed in writing, employees must observe their normal working hours, shifts and meal intervals, remain accessible to management, customers and relevant stakeholders, and record attendance through the prescribed remote clock-in and clock-out method. Failure to record attendance may be treated as unauthorised absence. Employees must record hours and completed tasks in the Bank’s online database and meet measurable performance, responsiveness and productivity requirements. Bank-owned or approved personal equipment may be used subject to prior approval, security controls and the Remote Work Agreement. Confidential or restricted information may not be removed from the primary workplace or accessed remotely without advance approval. Loss, damage, system compromise, unauthorised access and work-related injuries must be reported immediately. The policy limits the Bank’s liability to injuries arising from authorised remote work within agreed hours and the approved workspace. Each division must appoint a Remote Work Administrator, while remote work is administered by the Human Resources function in consultation with line management. Written exception requests require recommendation by the relevant Deputy General Manager and approval from the Deputy General Manager responsible for Human Resources and subsequently the Corporate Management Committee. Compliance may be periodically reviewed through sampled documents and records. Failure to comply may result in disciplinary action, including termination of service. The policy is subject to annual review, with the next revision scheduled for July 2026.

Operation Instruction Circular No. 16/2016, issued by the Bank of Ceylon on 20 June 2016, defines the responsibilities of branches in recovering credit card liabilities and reducing the volume of non-performing BOC credit card accounts. The circular applies to Branch Managers, Credit Officers, Recovery Officers, branch staff and the BOC Card Centre. Its objective is to ensure that credit card liabilities are identified and appropriately dealt with when branches process new loans, restructure or reschedule existing facilities, recover debts, release securities or close customer accounts. When processing a fresh loan, the outstanding credit card balance must be included in the Proposed Limit Summary, and the BOC card account must remain in the regular segment with status “NORM” before the customer qualifies for a new facility. For restructuring of a regular facility, the Branch Manager must confirm that the card account is regular and carries “NORM” status; otherwise, the cardholder must regularize it by paying at least the Minimum Due Amount. When rescheduling a non-performing advance, the card balance, accrued interest and any Easy Payment Plan balance must be included in Reschedule Loans “A” and “B” and secured by available collateral value. The prescribed accounting entries require the relevant amounts to be debited to the Loan Receivable account and credited separately to the Mail Transfer Payable Account of the Credit Card Centre, with the card number and cardholder name as the description. Where a loan applicant appears in CRIB for a BOC credit card, the total outstanding and accrued interest must be fully recovered and the card reactivated or closed before processing the facility. For cards issued by other banks, clearance from the relevant financial institution is required. Branches must recover credit card minimum dues alongside monthly loan obligations and include card balances in settlement negotiations and proceeds from disposal of mortgaged property. Security documents must not be released without referring to the Credit Card Centre. Cash deposits assigned against cards must remain assigned until full settlement and cancellation, and may support another facility only where the deposit exceeds 130 per cent of the approved card limit. Credit card dues must also be addressed before closing joint accounts, releasing withheld funds or lodging claims following a cardholder’s death. Compliance is mandatory, although no formal appeal process or separate reporting deadline is specified.

BOC-ITD-PMO-SOP-001, Project Numbering Procedure, Version 1.0, is an internal Bank of Ceylon Information Technology Division Standard Operating Procedure effective from 24 July 2024. Its objective is to establish a consistent method for classifying IT projects, assigning unique project names and numbers, maintaining a central Project Register, changing project information and formally closing project numbers. The procedure applies to permanent and contract employees, contractors, consultants, vendors, suppliers, partners and customers involved in Bank of Ceylon IT project management. The main implementation tools are a Microsoft Excel-based central Project Register and Microsoft Project Server. Each project number contains three components: a three-letter uppercase code identifying the division within the Business Group, a two-digit year in which the project began and a two-digit sequential number. For example, SFD-23-05 represents the Strategy and Finance Division, a project started in 2023 and the fifth project registered during that year. During project initiation, the Project Manager must obtain an approved Project Request Form and email the Project Management Office requesting a project number, together with the Purchase Initiation and Approval Form and details including priority, division, project name, type, description, budget, start date, sponsor and relevant project managers. The PMO checks the Project Register for duplicate projects and, where applicable, validates the request against the Bank’s business or departmental strategic plan. The PMO allocates the number, records the project and informs the Project Manager, who must then notify relevant stakeholders. Changes to a project number, name, description or schedule require an email request with justification. The PMO may accept, defer or reject the request after consulting relevant stakeholders. Project closure requires the Project Manager to request sign-off of the closure document, after which the PMO verifies the registration status, updates the register, transfers the project from active to closed status, records the closure date and informs the Project Manager. Monthly live-project and closed-project reports must be submitted to the IT Steering Committee, while an annual running list of closed projects must be retained. The procedure does not prescribe financial penalties, appeal rights, interest rates, repayment obligations or recovery actions. Compliance is supported through defined responsibilities, project-registration monitoring and alignment with ISO, ITIL, COBIT and PMBOK practices.

BOC-ITD-PMO-SOP-003, Project Risk Management Procedure, Version 1.0, is an internal Bank of Ceylon Information Technology Division Standard Operating Procedure effective from 24 July 2024. Its objective is to establish a structured, proactive process for identifying, assessing, responding to, monitoring and closing project risks throughout the full IT project lifecycle. The procedure applies to all ITD Project Managers performing project management activities and assigns supporting responsibilities to the Project Management Office, Project Sponsor, Chairperson of the Project Steering Committee, Business Project Manager, Vendor Project Manager and Chief Information Security Officer. The prescribed tools are the Risk Management Plan, Project Risk Register, Project Progress Reports and Project Portfolio Report. During project initiation, the Project Manager must identify and assess available risks with project-team members and stakeholders, develop mitigation plans and obtain approval from the Project Steering Committee Chairperson or Sponsor, which may be provided by email. Approved risks must be entered in the Project Risk Register, which remains active until project completion. Risks may be opened, updated and closed throughout the project lifecycle. The Project Manager must distinguish high-level risks from technical or project-management-specific risks and copy significant risks into Section 9 of the Project Charter so they are acknowledged by the Charter signatories. During planning, the Project Manager must prepare a Project Risk Management Plan as part of the overall Project Management Plan. It must cover the risk-management methodology, roles and responsibilities, mitigation budget, timing, risk categories, probability and impact definitions, risk-rating matrix, reporting formats and scheduled risk-review meetings. The plan requires approval from the Project Steering Committee Chairperson, Sponsor or another applicable approving party. During implementation and closure, risk identification, assessment, mitigation and register updates must continue regularly. New risks must be included in Project Progress Reports, while risks may be closed where assessment supports closure. At project completion, risk-management records must be disposed of or retained in accordance with project-document storage policies and procedures. Key controls include the high-level risk list in the Project Charter, an updated register of open and closed risks and a risk section in each Project Progress Report. Monitoring covers unresolved Critical, High and Medium risks, the age of outstanding risks, delays caused by unresolved risks and the final risk status at project closure. No fixed submission deadline, penalty, appeal procedure, interest rate, repayment obligation or recovery action is specified.

BOC-ITD-POL-015, Management Responsibilities Policy, Version 1.0, is an internal Bank of Ceylon Information Technology Division policy effective from 7 August 2024. Its objective is to establish the responsibilities and leadership commitments of IT Division Management in relation to the establishment, implementation, operation, monitoring, review, maintenance and continuous improvement of the Division’s Management System, including its Quality Management and IT Service Management components. The policy applies specifically to all members of the IT Management Committee and expressly excludes other ITD staff from its direct scope. Management is required to establish Quality and IT Service Management policies and objectives, conduct regular management reviews, determine and satisfy customer requirements, improve customer satisfaction and appoint a Management Representative to oversee the Management Systems. Management must also provide adequate resources for implementation, operation, monitoring, maintenance and improvement; ensure internal audits are conducted at the defined frequency; manage Quality and Service Management risks; establish effective internal communication processes; review the effectiveness of implemented policies; and motivate ITD staff to comply with organizational policies and procedures. The policy does not prescribe a specific operational implementation deadline beyond its effective date, nor does it establish external reporting obligations. Requests for exceptions must be submitted in writing and reviewed individually. Any exception must be approved in accordance with the Information Technology and Information Security Exception Request Policy. Compliance with management responsibilities is subject to periodic audit by the IT Governance Unit. Where an individual violates the policy, the IT Management Committee must be notified, and the matter may be reviewed for further action. The policy itself does not define specific penalties, disciplinary measures or an appeal process. It must be reviewed annually by the policy owner, whenever the policy or associated tool is updated, or when requested by the IT Management Committee. The policy references the Bank’s Quality Manual and IT Service Management Manual and aligns management responsibilities with ISO 9001, ISO 10002, ISO 27001, ISO 20000, ITIL V3 and COBIT 2019 governance and leadership provisions. The document was approved through the Review Committee, Implementation Committee, Project Steering Committee, ICT Committee and Board of Directors. Overall, it establishes management accountability for effective governance, customer focus, resource allocation, risk oversight, audit and continual improvement of IT management systems.

BOC-ITD-POL-044, eMail Usage Policy, Version 1.0, is an internal Bank of Ceylon Information Technology Division policy effective from 4 September 2024. Its objective is to support efficient official communication while preventing illegal, inappropriate, insecure or resource-intensive use of the Bank's email facilities. The policy applies to all Bank staff and employees, and every user of Bank IT facilities is responsible for protecting the Bank's systems and the information accessed, transmitted or manipulated through email. The IT Division must apply a standardized confidentiality and legal disclaimer to external messages, regularly review and monitor the email service, maintain current antivirus protection and filter suspicious or malicious attachments at the gateway. Messages containing detected viruses may be deleted, with an automated warning issued to the sender or recipient. Incoming attachments are monitored for excessive file size, known viruses and Trojan-related risks. Employees needing to send or receive large files must contact the IT Service Desk and should use links to internal network folders rather than large attachments for internal communication. Users must not engage in spamming, letter bombing, chain-letter distribution, hate or propaganda circulation, impersonation, or transmission of confidential, political, obscene, threatening, offensive, defamatory or libellous material. They must avoid profanity and derogatory remarks concerning employees, customers, competitors or others and must not use email in a way that interferes with computing facilities or other users. Information-security alerts, suspected vulnerabilities and unusual activities must be reported promptly to the IT Service Desk. Each user receives an initial mailbox quota of 1 GB. Additional storage requires approval from the Head of IT Infrastructure and Operations and may be increased by no more than 500 MB. Users must check email regularly, verify recipients, use meaningful subject lines, delete unnecessary messages and respect copyright and licensing requirements. Blocked business-related messages may be released through the IT Service Desk after authorization. Accidental access to prohibited material must be reported under the Information Security Incident Reporting Procedure. Exceptions must be requested in writing and handled under the Information Technology and Information Security Exception Request Policy. The Process Owner is responsible for assessing compliance, and the policy must be reviewed annually, when updated or at the request of the IT Management Committee. No specific disciplinary penalty, appeal process, repayment requirement, recovery action or interest provision is stated.

BOC-ITD-SOP-001, Document Control Procedure, Version 1.0, is an internal Bank of Ceylon Information Technology Division procedure effective from 24 July 2024. Its objective is to establish a controlled method for creating, reviewing, approving, distributing, maintaining, changing, storing and retiring Management System documentation. It covers the Quality Management System under ISO 9001:2015, customer-satisfaction management under ISO 10002:2018 and the IT Service Management System under ISO 20000-1:2018. The procedure applies to permanent and contract employees, contractors, consultants, vendors, suppliers, partners and customers involved in IT document usage. All internally produced and externally supplied documents must be uniquely identified and subjected to version control to prevent duplication and ensure that only current, approved versions are used. New documents begin at Version 1.0. Minor procedural or worksheet changes increment the decimal version, while major changes increment the main version number and require approval by the Designated Approval Authority. Documents are stored in the access-controlled IT Governance shared folder and published through the BOC eLearning Portal on a need-to-know basis. The Management Representative maintains the Document Master List. Only the document or process owner may initiate changes, which must be recorded in the IT Process Change Request Form, reviewed through the defined cycle and approved by the Information Technology Management Committee or applicable authority. Post-facto changes must be presented at the immediately following ITMC meeting. Authors circulate completed documents for review by relevant Section or Unit Heads. After corrections are resolved, the document is sent to the Designated Approval Authority, which obtains Internal Audit consent before approval. Rejected documents cannot be published until reworked. Once approved, the Document Controller updates the effective date, publishes the document and the IT Governance Unit sends an email notification containing the title and URL to ITD staff. Reader access may be restricted to viewing only, without printing, downloading or modification. External-origin documents, including contracts, business cases, agreements, personal data and intellectual property, must be identified, verified, protected and safeguarded. Key controls include review and approval, master-list control, change management, retention, storage and removal of obsolete documents. No acknowledgement deadline, financial penalty, appeal process, interest provision, repayment obligation or recovery action is specified.

Office Instructions Circular No. 32/2017 establishes the Bank of Ceylon’s operating framework for Personal Foreign Currency Accounts in accordance with Directions No. 03 of 2017 issued under the Foreign Exchange Act No. 12 of 2017. Its objective is to implement the newly introduced PFCA product and redesignate existing NRFC, RFC, RNNFC, NRNNFA and eligible FCAISPE-Employee Accounts as PFCAs with effect from 20 November 2017. The circular applies to all branches and concerned departments. Eligible parties include Sri Lankan nationals, including minors; persons of Sri Lankan origin residing outside Sri Lanka; non-nationals resident in Sri Lanka; non-nationals temporarily visiting or intending to visit Sri Lanka; and administrators or executors of deceased PFCA holders until estate administration is completed. PFCAs may be maintained as savings, current or term-deposit accounts in designated foreign currencies. PFCA current accounts cannot be issued cheque books or overdrawn. Accounts may generally be held solely or jointly, although temporary or prospective non-national visitors cannot hold joint PFCAs. Branches must obtain an application, KYC form, identification and, where applicable, signature cards, passport, visa, employment, salary, foreign-address and residential-status information. Eligibility evidence must be retained during the account relationship and for six years after closure. Savings and current accounts may be opened with zero balance and are not subject to service charges. Permitted credits include inward foreign remittances, declared foreign-currency notes, transfers from specified foreign-currency and investment accounts, eligible migrant income and benefits, investment proceeds, travel-card balances and foreign-currency interest. Currency notes of USD 15,000 or more require Customs declaration, while lower amounts require a declaration to the Bank. Permitted debits include outward remittances, rupee disbursements, eligible account transfers, travel-card loading, investments, transfers to loan accounts and foreign-currency withdrawals for travel up to USD 10,000. Residential status must be recorded in ICBS User Field 15, and transfer descriptions must identify the relevant account type and account number. Head Office must submit the Annexure VII monthly return to the Central Bank’s Department of Foreign Exchange by the fifteenth day of the following month. PFCA fixed deposits require an underlying PFCA savings account and a minimum deposit of USD 1,000 or equivalent. Premature withdrawals held over one month incur a 0.25 per cent annual deduction from the applicable completed-month rate; deposits held for less than one month earn no interest. No penalties, appeal process or recovery procedures are specified.
